# Rational Design, Synthesis, and Biological Evaluation of Novel c-Met Degraders for Lung Cancer Therapy

Xingyang Qiu,§ Qingquan Zheng,§ Dongdong Luo,§ Yue Ming, Tingting Zhang, Wenchen Pu, Min Ai, Jianhua He, and Yong Peng*

Cite This: J. Med. Chem. 2025, 68, 2815–2839

Read Online

## ACCESS

Metrics & More

Article Recommendations

Supporting Information

**ABSTRACT:** Cellular-mesenchymal epithelial transition factor (c-Met) is an attractive target for treating multiple cancers. Despite plentiful c-Met inhibitors have been developed, some issues, including the acquired drug resistance to c-Met inhibitors, have emerged to hamper their application in clinical treatment. Degradation of c-Met offers an opportunity to solve these issues. In this study, we developed a series of c-Met degraders, and the optimal compound 22b can efficiently degrade c-Met with a DC₅₀ value of 0.59 nM in EBC-1 cells. Mechanistic studies revealed that compound 22b induced c-Met degradation via proteasome-mediated pathway. In addition, compound 22b suppressed the proliferation and also induced apoptosis of EBC-1 cells, outperforming the corresponding inhibitor tepotinib. Importantly, compound 22b showed favorable pharmacokinetic properties and significantly induced tumor regression in a xenograft model without obvious toxicity. In brief, this study provided compound 22b as a novel c-Met degrader for lung cancer therapy.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

## INTRODUCTION

Receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) comprise nearly 60 members,¹ most of which have been identified as potential targets for the treatment of various diseases, including inflammation,² cancer,³,⁴ diabetes,⁵ immune deficiency,⁶ and cardiovascular disease.⁷,⁸ As a member of RTKs, cellular-mesenchymal epithelial transition factor (c-Met) is an attractive target in cancer therapy and c-Met inhibitors have shown promising clinical benefits in nonsmall cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and kidney cancer with many drugs already marketed,⁹ such as crizotinib, tepotinib, capmatinib, and savolitinib.¹⁰⁻¹⁴ As shown in Figure 1A, c-Met inhibitors mainly contain three types (type I, II, III inhibitors) distinguished by binding patterns between inhibitors and the c-Met kinase domain. Both type I and II inhibitors are ATP-competitive inhibitors and type III inhibitors are non-ATP-competitive as allosteric inhibitors. Type I inhibitors commonly adopt U-shaped conformations to bind the entrance of the kinase pocket and wrap around Met1211 residue, type II inhibitors bind to the c-Met kinase domain at the ATP-binding site with an extended conformation, which stretches into a hydrophobic pocket near the C-helix part. Type I inhibitors are further divided into type Ia and type Ib depending on whether there is hydrophobic interaction between inhibitors and Gly1163 residue of c-Met kinase domain. Gly1163 residue is near the solvent region and interacts with hydrophobic fragments of Type Ia inhibitors by hydrophobic interactions, which are conducive to the binding

between inhibitors and the c-Met kinase domain. In contrast, the bind of type Ib inhibitors and the c-Met kinase domain are independent of these interactions with Gly1163 residue, resulting in type Ib inhibitors being more selective than type Ia inhibitors.⁹ For instance, in addition to blocking c-Met with an IC₅₀ value of 8 nM, crizotinib (a representative type Ia c-Met inhibitor, first approval by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 2011 for the therapy of advanced ALK-positive NSCLC) also showed strong inhibitory activity against ALK and ROS1 kinases with IC₅₀ value of the low nanomolar range.¹⁵⁻¹⁷ While Type Ib inhibitors, such as tepotinib, capmatinib, and savolitinib, not only showed favorable c-Met inhibitory activity (IC₅₀ < 10 nM) but also are >200-fold selective for c-Met than other kinases (e.g., IRAK4, AXL, and IRAK1).⁹ Type II inhibitors (cabozantinib, foretinib, and merestinib) generally also inhibit other kinases (e.g., ALK, AXL, and VEGFR-2), depending on the substitution patterns of inhibitors.¹⁸⁻²⁰ Type III inhibitors are allosteric inhibitors and an example is tivantinib which binds the allosteric pocket of the c-Met kinase domain with a Kᵢ value of 355 nM.²¹ Despite various c-Met inhibitors have
